hypotheses: H1. A negative correlation will be found between secure attachment style and a high level of trait anxiety.
H2. A positive correlation will be found between secure attachment style and openness to experiences.
H3. A negative relation will be found between levels of trait anxiety and leadership experiences.
H4. A positive relation will be found between openness to experiences and leadership experiences.
claim.hypotheses_location: Page 752, right column, under section beginning 'The two preliminary hypotheses...'; Page 752, paragraphs introducing H3 and H4.
claim.statement: Secure attachment formed in early childhood predicts lower trait anxiety and higher openness to experience, which in turn foster leadership development via leadership experiences.
claim.statement_location: Abstract (page 749) and Figure 1 caption description on page 752.
claim.study_type: Observational (cross-sectional survey with structural equation modeling)

data.source: Self-administered questionnaires completed by Israel Defense Forces soldiers during basic training
data.wave_or_subset: Single data collection during basic training across four companies (two armoured, two infantry)
data.notes: Responses from 10-20% new immigrants with language difficulties were excluded; final analytic Ns range 236-245 across variables because of missing data.

method.description: Male soldiers nearing the end of basic training completed four validated questionnaires measuring adult attachment style, trait anxiety, openness to experience and leadership-shaping experiences. Items for each construct were parceled and analysed with structural equation modeling (SEM) in AMOS 5. Multiple alternative causal models were compared using goodness-of-fit indices.
steps: Recruit 286 male IDF soldiers from four basic-training companies who volunteered to participate.
Administer paper questionnaires: 13-item adult attachment (anxiety & avoidance), 20-item Trait Anxiety Inventory, 24-item Openness to Experience scale, 7-item Leadership-Shaping Experiences scale.
Reverse-score where required; compute total scores.
Randomly parcel items of each scale into three indicators per latent construct.
Specify measurement model and assess fit (χ², CFI, NFI, NNFI, GFI, RMSEA).
Estimate structural model (Model 1) with secure attachment → trait anxiety & openness; these → leadership experiences. Record standardized path coefficients and R².
Test five alternative structural models, compare fit statistics and chi-square difference tests.
method.models: Structural Equation Modeling (maximum likelihood estimation in AMOS 5)
method.independent_variables: Secure attachment style, Trait anxiety, Openness to experience

results.summary: The hypothesised model fit the data well (χ²(50)=57.3, p=.22, CFI=.99, RMSEA=.02). Secure attachment negatively predicted trait anxiety (β=-0.62, p<.01) and positively predicted openness to experience (β=0.24, p<.01). Trait anxiety negatively predicted leadership experiences (β=-0.23, p<.01) whereas openness positively predicted leadership experiences (β=0.34, p<.01). The model explained 19% of the variance in leadership experiences. Alternative models adding direct paths from attachment or anxiety to leadership experiences did not improve fit.

metadata.original_paper_title: Attachment and leader's development via experiences
